steps.dialogue: [event:show_lines][event:69617705c1cb1626]Take a look at these three different fractions. You might see a pattern.

metadata.notes: This and the step before it are both broken until we get a way to shade intervals. Aleks says that will happen soon as of 1/9/26

steps.dialogue: Each one shows counting all the intervals from 0. When we count all the intervals, [event:697bb65274819f62]3 out of 3,[d:1] [event:697bb697849e79c2]4 out of 4, [d:1] [event:697bb6a95c2b531f]6 out of 6,[d:1] what do we get?

prompt.text: What do all the intervals in each number line equal?

step.dialogue: Remember, we're thinking about all of the intervals in each line.
Not quite. Remember,  [event:697bf2a38e537bcd]when we count all of the intervals, we have the whole line.

Watch:  [event:697bf537d80ca40d]3 parts out of 3 total = the whole thing[d:0.5] = 1. Same for [event:697bf6ba3d83b4a3][fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction], and [event:697bf70c502a9da8][fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Right. They [event:697bb7b0d0a78986]all equal ONE whole. When the top number equals the bottom number, we always get 1.

steps.dialogue: [event:6917a7d0558ed24f]Let's explore this pattern. If [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction] equals 1, what comes next?

prompt.text: Select what fraction comes after [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction]

step.dialogue: The [event:synthesis_2_md] spaces stay the same size, they're still thirds. What's one more third past [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction]?

Follow along with me:  We have thirds. After [event:697c0a6b089885c7][fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction] comes [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3] [/fraction] [event:synthesis_2_hv] four thirds.

on_correct.dialogue: Yes! [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]four thirds[/fraction], [event:6917a5bd44a188f8] one more third past the whole.

steps.dialogue: After [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]four thirds[/fraction] comes [event:695d502d0283140d] [fraction numerator=5 denominator=3]five thirds [/fraction] and then[event:695d50756a396386] [fraction numerator=6 denominator=3]six thirds[/fraction] which equals 2!
[event:show_lines]Remember from before when you built fractions by combining unit fractions? Now you see where those fractions live!
Each  [event:697c0bb6d37eaa29]denominator tells us how many equal spaces to make between 0 and 1. [event:6910f34689d4bc41] Bigger denominators mean more spaces, so each space is smaller.

The more you work with fractions, the more you are understanding what the numbers really mean. The [event:697c0ccb406664c7]denominator tells us how [event:6910f4ffeded4d77] many equal spaces there are on the line. And every fraction has its [event:6910f466c9d78a50] exact position on that line!

Nice effort today! You're learning that fractions are numbers with specific places on the number line. And when we count up all the pieces, like [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] or [fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction], we always get one whole.
Next time, you'll learn to place any fraction with precision. You've worked hard today, and I think you are ready! Your work today has helped you understand what denominators really do!
